exemplar.title: To what extent has Samsung violated its ethical codes, and how has it affected its brand image?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent has Samsung violated its ethical codes, and how has it affected its brand image?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear explanation of ethics with repeated links to Samsungâs labour controversies
Ethical concept frames major analytical tools (SWOT, STEEPLE, survey) consistently
Samsung remains the focus throughout, with most sections connecting back to its ethical record
weaknesses: Lapses into narrative description without explicitly ethical critique at times
No concise definition or operationalisation of ethics to guide analysis
Integration is partial rather than sustained across every section
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place this work solidly in band 3: good understanding of the ethical concept with clear analytical thinking and a generally strong link to Samsung. However, the analysis occasionally reverts to description without probing the concept, and there is no fully continuous integration across all tools (e.g., ethical framing in economic factor discussion). A score of 3/5 accurately reflects âgood but not exceptionalâ integration.

strengths: Four sources listed (within the 3â5 ideal range) fulfilling the quantitative requirement
Documents are diverse and directly related to Samsungâs ethical record
Labelled supporting documents and appendix improve traceability
weaknesses: Some sources lack summaries or evaluation of their content (e.g., hyperlink-only entry)
Depth is moderateâsummaries are descriptive rather than critically mined
The direct contribution of certain documents remains unclear due to missing integration
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission meets the basic quantitative requirement (band 2) but only achieves moderate depth. While sources are relevant, they are not consistently evaluated or integrated. Thus a 2/4 reflects âmarginally more than minimalâ use of documents without the in-depth critical engagement needed for higher bands.

strengths: Selection of SWOT, STEEPLE, Force-Field and CSR/utilitarian theory aligns with syllabus and research question
SWOT table and utilitarian theory discussion are clearly defined and appropriately cited
Tools are applied to the research question with relevance to Samsungâs ethics
weaknesses: Application is sometimes descriptive, with causal links asserted rather than substantiated
More advanced models (e.g., stakeholder mapping, Porterâs Five Forces) are absent
Methodology description omits sample-size rationale and analysis procedures
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Band 3 describes an adequate selection of tools and reasonably clear application to the research question. The student demonstrates syllabus-linked tool choice and relevance, but uneven critical rigour and missing sophisticated models prevent a higher band. A score of 3/4 is appropriate.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Purposeful selection of lawsuit timelines, CSR statements and primary survey data
Incorporation of third-party assessments (Ethical Consumer ratings) and compensation figures adds empirical weight
Visual aids (pie charts) support data presentation
weaknesses: Analysis often remains superficial; claims (e.g., revenue decline) lack quantitative backing
Survey sample (n=5) is too small to generalise and lacks statistical reliability
Evaluation restates tool findings without synthesising into strategic recommendations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although there is some purposeful use of data and tool outputs, the analysis is uneven and not consistently sustained. Key claims lack quantitative support, and the small survey sample undermines evaluative strength. This aligns with band 2 (âsome analysis of the research questionâ) rather than band 3, justifying a 2/5.

strengths: Conclusions restate main findings and identify key ethical breaches
Recommendations logically follow from preceding analysis
weaknesses: Conclusion cuts off mid-sentence and lacks explicit measurement of impact
Degree and extent of ethical violations are asserted rather than demonstrated
No quantitative evidence included to substantiate conclusions on sales or brand impact
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion shows some consistency with evidence but fails to fully answer 'to what extent' and is incomplete in places. This performance matches band 2 (partial answer, some evidence consistency), warranting 2/3.

strengths: Clear organization matching the table of contents (Introduction, Methodology, Findings, Analysis, Conclusion)
weaknesses: Executive summary is not clearly distinguished as a standalone section
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission displays an appropriate and coherent structure throughout, satisfying band 2. Minor flaws (e.g., unlabelled executive summary) do not undermine overall organization, supporting a 2/2.

strengths: Includes all required IB IA elements: cover page with RQ and word count, table of contents, citations, appendices
Appendices and labelled documents aid clarity
weaknesses: Table of contents lacks page numbers
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All required elements are present, meeting the highest band for presentation. Formatting inconsistencies are minor and do not remove required components. A full 2/2 is warranted.

strandSelections.reasoning: A logical sequenceâIntroduction, Methodology, Findings, Analyses, Evaluation, Conclusion, Appendixâruns through the paper, mirroring the Table of Contents.  Headings and sub-headings aid navigation, evidencing appropriate structure throughout.

The Britannica profile, Business & Human Rights case file, Ethical Consumer report and academic essay all relate directly to Samsungâs ethical record and provide varied perspectives.  Depth is moderateâeach document is summarised rather than critically mined, but they are clearly pertinent and inform later discussion.  This aligns with ârelevant and sufficiently in-depth.â

The submission includes a cover section with RQ and word count, table of contents, citations/works-cited list, appendices with survey data and charts, and labelled supporting documents.  These meet IB IA presentation requirements, so all required elements appear present.
Four supporting sources are explicitly listed and labelled, keeping the total within the 3-5 range, thus satisfying the quantitative requirement of the 2-mark band.  No excess or deficit is present.

Tools are applied to the research questionâfor instance, SWOT contrasts ethical weaknesses with brand-loyalty strengths, and STEEPLEâs ethical element is linked to revenue loss.  Still, application often remains descriptive; causal chains (ethics â image â sales) are asserted with little data support.  Relevance is clear but depth and critical rigour are uneven, fitting band 3.

Data include multiple lawsuit timelines, CSR statements and a primary survey (5 responses, pie charts).  Though largely qualitative and small-scale, these sources are chosen purposefully to address ethical breaches and image effects, satisfying âadequate selection and use.â  There is room for more robust quantitative evidence to reach higher bands.

Analysis interprets how ethics controversies influence brand image, uses SWOT/STEEPLE findings to weigh impacts, and evaluates CSR responses.  Connections are drawn, but reasoning is sometimes superficial (e.g., claiming revenue fell drastically without figures).  Integration is partial and not consistently sustained, matching the âsome analysisâ descriptor of band 3.

The research question is partially answered: the student concludes that Samsung violated ethics âmultiple timesâ and outlines brand-image effects, but degree (âto what extentâ) is not clearly measured and impact on sales is asserted rather than demonstrated.  Thus a partial, not explicit, answer.

The student selects SWOT, STEEPLE, Force-Field and basic CSR/Utilitarian theory.  These cover internal, external and ethical analytical lenses, constituting an adequate, syllabus-linked toolkit, yet more sophisticated models (e.g., stakeholder mapping, Porter, marketing-mix) are absent.  Hence âadequateâ rather than âeffective.â

The conclusion restates key findings and references responsibility for long working hours but offers limited citation of evidence, and no quantitative validation of revenue impact.  Consistency exists in broad terms yet lacks supporting depth, aligning with âsome conclusions consistent with evidence.â

Samsung is kept in focus throughout: background, lawsuits, CSR responses, survey and analytical tools all relate directly to the firm.  The connection is clearly analysed in most sections (e.g., discussion of revenue impact and consumer perceptions).  Nevertheless, links between individual data points and broader ethical implications for Samsung are sometimes asserted rather than examined, so the integration is only partial rather than sustained.  This best matches band 3.

The student explains the meaning of ethics and repeatedly links the term to Samsungâs labour-related controversies.  Ethical considerations are referenced in every major section and are used to frame the survey, SWOT and STEEPLE discussions, showing a reasonable analytical grasp.  However, many passages lapse into narrative description of lawsuits without explicitly analysing them through an ethical lens, and there is no sustained, continuous integration of the concept across all tools.  This aligns with the descriptor for band 3: good understanding with clear, but not fully integrated, analytical thinking.

annotations.comment: The chronological account of the 2013 lawsuit is detailed and helps track the progression of ethical claims.
Legal timeline description is thorough but would benefit from direct ethical critique at each stage.
Only a hyperlink is provided for Supporting document 3 without integration or evaluation of its content.
Incorporation of Ethical Consumer ratings adds depth by showing external third-party assessments.
Including compensation data for executives offers a valuable counterpoint in assessing anti-social finance ethics.
The executive summary section is not clearly distinguished, making it hard to identify as a standalone summary.
The student clearly states the research question and focuses the investigation, aiding coherent direction throughout the analysis.
The label âKey concept: Ethicsâ is stated but lacks a concise definition or operationalisation to guide analytical application throughout the paper.
Including the word count prominently meets IB IA presentation requirements and aids transparency.
The table of contents is comprehensive and well-structured, enhancing navigation through the sections.
Page numbers are missing from the table of contents entries, which could hinder quick reference to specific sections.
The introduction provides background and context, but it relies heavily on narrative and would benefit from tighter linkage to the ethical concept.
The historical overview of Samsung is detailed and situates the ethical inquiry within the companyâs evolution, strengthening the analysis.
Methodology description refers to surveys but omits sample size rationale and analysis procedures, reducing methodological clarity.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Descriptions of early lawsuits are informative but remain largely descriptive without probing the ethical implications explicitly.
The student provides specific case details (dates, allegations, outcomes), which supports evidence-based discussion.
The brand image section asserts a drastic revenue drop without providing supporting quantitative data, weakening the claim.
Consider integrating actual financial figures or survey results to substantiate the effect of ethics on revenue and brand image.
The SWOT table is an appropriate tool choice, capturing internal strengths and weaknesses relevant to ethics.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: SWOT entries remain descriptive; deeper evaluation of how strengths may mitigate ethical threats would improve critical rigour.
The STEEPLE social analysis effectively links consumer awareness to ethical brand pressure.
Economic factors are described but lack connection to the ethical research question, diluting conceptual integration.
The evaluation section restates findings from SWOT and STEEPLE but stops short of synthesising these into strategic recommendations.
The conclusion cuts off mid-sentence and fails to explicitly answer the extent of ethical violations and their impact on brand image.
A sample of only 5 survey responses is too small to generalise consumer perceptions meaningfully.
Survey results are presented without statistical analysis or discussion of reliability, limiting their evaluative value.
The survey pie chart visually supports primary data presentation and aids reader comprehension.
The follow-up survey question on brand change suggestions enriches insight into consumer expectations.
Work Cited includes diverse, relevant sources, demonstrating thorough document selection.
Citation formatting is inconsistent (e.g., missing access dates, varying styles), which may confuse readers.
Labelled supporting documents improve traceability and contextual understanding in the appendix.
Supporting document 2 section lacks a summary of its content, so its direct contribution remains unclear.
Contradictory data from the annual report is noted but not reconciled, which undermines the earlier claim of revenue decline.
The CSR discussion effectively connects the concept to the case and provides a sound theoretical lens.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Stakeholder section lacks a structured map or prioritisation to show how different groups influence Samsungâs ethical responsibilities.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Detailed findings from China Labor Watch strengthen the empirical basis, supporting ethical critique.
Post-audit brand image discussion lacks evidence of actual shifts in consumer perception following Samsungâs contractor reviews.
The recommendations logically follow from the analysis, providing clear steps for Samsung to enhance ethical standards.
The utilitarian theory is clearly defined with appropriate citation, strengthening theoretical grounding.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories
